metadata.title: Loan Applications & Creditworthiness
metadata.subtitle: Master the loan application process and understand how lenders evaluate your creditworthiness

content.title: Loan Applications & Creditworthiness
content.subtitle: Master the loan application process and understand how lenders evaluate your creditworthiness

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Carmen prepared ahead and secured a 5.8% interest rate on her auto loan, while Marcus rushed in unprepared and received a 22% rate. For the same loan amount over 5 years, Marcus paid approximately $2,000 more in interest.

How can preparation turn the same loan into vastly different financial outcomes?

slides.notes:
Discussion Prompts:
• Contrast Carmen (prepared, got 5.8% rate) vs. Marcus (unprepared, got 22% rate).
• Calculate the difference: ~$2,000 in extra interest over 5 years.
• Key insight: Same loan, VASTLY different outcomes based on preparation.

objectives.description: a comprehensive loan application with accuracy and required documentation
and gather necessary documentation for different loan types
how lenders use the Five C's to evaluate creditworthiness
strategies to strengthen loan applications and improve approval odds

terms.definition: A lender's assessment of your ability and willingness to repay debt, based on credit history, income, employment stability, and existing debts.
terms.example: High credit score + stable income = strong creditworthiness

terms.definition: The percentage of your gross monthly income that goes toward debt payments. Lenders prefer DTI below 36-43%.
An asset (car, house, savings) that secures a loan and can be seized if you fail to repay. Reduces lender risk and typically results in lower rates.
terms.example: Your car secures an auto loan; your home secures a mortgage

terms.definition: A preliminary evaluation where a lender reviews your finances and indicates willingness to lend up to a certain amount.
terms.example: Getting pre-approved for $280,000 before house hunting

layoutData.title: Lenders evaluate every loan application using these five key factors
bulletPoints: Character: Your credit history and track record of repaying debts
Capacity: Your ability to repay based on income and existing debts
Capital: Your savings, investments, and down payment available
Collateral: Assets that can secure the loan and reduce lender risk
Conditions: Loan purpose, economic environment, and circumstances

leftPanel.title: Understanding average rates helps you know if you're getting a good deal

infoCard.title: Why rates vary
items: Your credit score can shift these rates by 2-4 percentage points.
A 760+ score gets the best rates, while below 620 may add 3%+ to these averages.

content.headerTitle: How Interest Rates Affect Your Payments

rightColumn.title: Poor Credit (Below 620)
items: Difference: $6,720 more in interest with poor credit!

layoutData.title: Gather these documents before applying to streamline the process

scenario.name: Carmen's Prepared Auto Loan Application
paragraphs: Carmen researched financing 3 months before shopping. She checked her credit score (680), paid off an old medical bill, disputed a credit report error, and gathered all documentation.

When she found a $22,000 car, she was ready. Her credit union approved her at {{STATE_AVG_AUTO_LOAN_RATE_NEW}}% while the dealer offered 7.2%. Using her pre-approval, she negotiated the dealer down to match.

slides.notes:
Common Misconceptions:
MisconceptionClarification
"My credit score is the only thing that matters."Credit score (Character) is ONE of five factors. Someone with 800 score but 60% DTI will struggle. All Five C's matter.
"Checking my credit hurts my score."YOU checking your own credit (soft inquiry) doesn't affect score. Only lenders checking for applications (hard inquiries) have small, temporary impact.
"Higher income means automatic approval."Income affects Capacity, but high income with high debt still means high DTI. Lenders care about what percentage of income is available, not raw income amount.
"I should apply everywhere to find best rate."Multiple applications create multiple hard inquiries. Exception: Rate shopping for same loan type within 14-45 days often counts as single inquiry. Better: get pre-approved, THEN compare.

content.question:
What's the most important "C" of credit for a first-time borrower?

Consider: Young adults often lack credit history. How can they demonstrate creditworthiness? Which of the Five C's can they most easily influence?

content.headerTitle: Preparation Is the Key to Approval

layoutData.title: Key Insight
paragraphs: The loan application process isn't random—lenders use predictable criteria.
By understanding what they look for and preparing in advance, you can dramatically improve your approval odds and secure better terms.
layoutData.keyPoint: Success in loan applications comes from preparation, not luck.

takeaways.title: Improve Your Credit
takeaways.description: Pay down credit cards below 30% utilization. Every 20-point improvement can save 0.25-0.5% on rates.

takeaways.title: Lower Your DTI
takeaways.description: Pay off smaller debts before applying. This frees up monthly capacity for larger loans.

takeaways.title: Increase Down Payment
takeaways.description: Larger down payments reduce lender risk and can eliminate PMI on mortgages.

takeaways.title: Stay Employed
takeaways.description: Avoid job changes during applications. Lenders prefer 2+ years of stable employment.

takeaways.title: Get Pre-Approved
takeaways.description: Pre-approval provides negotiating leverage and clarifies your budget before shopping.

takeaways.title: Consider a Co-signer
takeaways.description: A co-signer with strong credit can help if your profile is weak—use carefully.

content.question:
Is a co-signer worth the risk?

If a co-signer helps you get approved, what obligations are created? When might it be better to wait and build your own credit instead?

leftColumn.title: What NOT to Do
items: Apply without checking your credit first
Accept the first offer without shopping
Change jobs during the application process

rightColumn.title: What TO Do
items: Check and improve credit 3-6 months before

takeaways.title: The Five C's matter
takeaways.description: Lenders evaluate Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, and Conditions consistently

takeaways.title: Preparation saves money
takeaways.description: Strong applications can save thousands in interest over a loan's life

takeaways.title: Documentation is critical
takeaways.description: Gathering documents early prevents delays and shows responsibility

takeaways.title: Shop around
takeaways.description: Compare rates from multiple lenders within a 14-45 day window to get the best deal

slides.notes:
Assessment Answer Key (Check Your Understanding - Slide 18):

Q1: What does DTI stand for, and why do lenders care about it?
Answer: DTI measures ability to afford the new payment. High DTI = too much income already committed to debt = higher risk of default because less buffer for unexpected expenses.

Q2: Name the Five C's of credit and explain which one you could most easily improve.
Answer: Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, and Conditions. Students should identify which they can most easily improve with specific actions (e.g., "Capacity by reducing DTI through paying off smaller debts").

Q3: If the average auto loan rate in {{STATE_NAME}} is {{STATE_AVG_AUTO_LOAN_RATE_NEW}}%, what might cause you to pay more?
Answer: Lower credit score (below 700), high DTI ratio, minimal down payment, limited credit history, or job instability. Each 20-point drop in credit score can add 0.25-0.5% to the rate.

Q4: Why is it smart to get pre-approved before shopping for a car or house?
Answer: Pre-approval shows sellers you're serious with financing ready (competitive advantage), clarifies your budget, lets you negotiate from strength (can walk away from dealer financing if rates are worse), and identifies issues early.
